paragraphs.text: THERE awhile back, who should hail us on a street of the roaring town but tall, young, broad-shouldered Lyn Atkinson, the man who built the great Coolidge Dam in Arizona in jig time and put it there to stay until Gabriel blows his horn.
Conductor, said this Lyn to us, Conductor of the Synagogue, he said, how would you like to voyage out to sea for a couple of days, or maybe three, or until the cows come home, in a ship that I have?

It was taking advantage of us. No time was at our disposal. The grindstone at home was waiting for our nose. Tethers were on our feet. Work upon which bread and butter depended stared us in the face. And, yet, with all that, here was this young fellow putting before us a temptation that we couldd not resist.

A ship, and the sea waiting for it. A bonnie ship with a skipper and a cook and everybody else that a ship should have with it. And the blood of long generations of sailormen in our veins. The old Phoenicians for ancestors. The grindstone faded from memory, and the tethers fell from our feet. And so, with the evening tide, we put out to sea.

Name your port and it is there that we shall sail, said the master of the ship. And then we saw an opportunity to fulfill and [an] old desire. Point her nose to the Isle of San Nicolas, we answered. And the master said aye, aye, sir.

It was of an early afternoon that we came upon the coast of San Nicolas, the sea sunny and quiet as a lake, a great flock of gray gulls and pelicans to welcome us, and seals bobbing up, as full of curiosity as the proverbial cat.

Now, in the old books of history and legend, you will see it said that the Isle San Nicolas is the loneliest and most desolate spot on all God's round earth. It was so spoken of by the earliest voyagers and is so spoken of still. And it is, in truth, one of the very loneliest of all places. Yet, shining there in the sun, and the wide blue sea around it, that Sunday morning, it seemed only as something very beautiful to us.

We could discern no sign of human life anywhere. Our ship had to stand well out to sea because of the vast beds of kelp that surrounded the island. And, besides, there is not the ghost of a harbor, even, on all its thirty miles of shore lines. But, suddenly through the glass, the master did see signs of life. Down to the shore a man came running, waving his hands in signal.

It is Robinson Crusoe, himself we said. And so, in great haste the captain and the sailors lowered a launch and we pushed through the winding channels of kelp to a shallow crescent of beach to greet the apparition.

But he was no apparition at all. Instead he was a man from far-off Maine whose home now is the island where he dwells with a famlly, a woman of Nebraska for wife, a lovely blue-eyed child whose name is Frances, and a Phoenician of the name of Daly who helps with the sheep that pasture on the bleak, windswept hills.
We had a happy day with them all, and the honor of their company for dinner on the ship. And all the tales of the island they knew were told to us.

We came at last to the one great story that San Nicolas has to tell—the story of the forgotten woman who lived there alone through eighteen years of what must have been as deep a tragedy as the world has ever known. A tale long familiar to us, and to the scene of which we had now at last come.
We could think of little else as the bright day fled. Of little else than this brown woman left alone on that loneliest of isles through the long, dragging, desolate, tragic years.

The way it was, the Franciscan Padres of the Santa Barbara Mission, long ago, learning that there was a small tribe of Indians on San Nicolas, sent a boat to bring them to the Mission where they could be baptized in the Christian faith and bent to the uses of civilization.
The feat was duly accomplished. But, as the boat was about to sail, this woman discovered that her baby had been left behind. A storm was brewing. The sailors would not wait.
So, the woman plunged into the sea, swam to the shore and was left behind, not to be remembered again until eighteen years had passed.

Then they found her. She had built a hut of whale bones covered with grass, and had woven a garb for her body from the feathers of birds. The baby she had risked her own life to save had died when still a baby. Then the eighteen years of a completely solitary life went on for her. Never a human voice in her ears, no human hand to touch, no companion save the birds and the animals of the sea. The same silent, voiceless sky above her day after day. Lost and desolate, with only God to know.

They brought her then to the Mission at Santa Barbara, poured the waters of Jordan on her head, and in one short month laid her in a quiet grave among the many graves that are there and which you can see when you go. A grave that was less lonely, indeed, than the living grave where she had apent those lonely, tragic years on San Nicolas.
We submit this tale to women of the Synagogue wherever they may be and who think their lot is hard. Let them consider the woman of San Nicolas.
